event: The Assumption of the Virgin Mary
symbols: outstretched arms of Mary — total surrender and openness to God, echoing the orant posture of prayer
golden radiance above — the glory of God receiving Mary into heaven
blue mantle — traditional Marian symbol of heavenly grace and fidelity
red/pink garment — the humanity and charity of Mary
putti and cherubim — the heavenly host bearing the Mother of God upward
clouds — the boundary between earth and heaven, the liminal space of theophany
upward gaze — Mary's eyes fixed on God, the goal of all human pilgrimage
description: The Virgin Mary ascends into heaven at the center of the composition, her arms spread wide and her face turned upward toward a brilliant golden light that radiates from above. She wears a flowing pink-red gown beneath a deep blue mantle that billows dramatically around her. Below her feet, several winged putti and cherub heads support her on a bed of clouds. The background is a warm golden-brown atmosphere with additional small angelic faces visible in the upper corners. The painting is vertical and monumental in feeling, with Mary's figure dominating the entire canvas.
theological_description: The Assumption of the Virgin is one of the most celebrated Marian doctrines, holding that Mary, at the end of her earthly life, was assumed body and soul into heavenly glory. While not narrated directly in Scripture, the doctrine draws on deep biblical typology. Mary is the new Ark of the Covenant (Revelation 11:19-12:1), and just as the Ark was brought up to its resting place in the Temple with great rejoicing (2 Chronicles 5:2-7; Psalm 132:8), so the living Ark is taken up into the heavenly sanctuary. The woman of Revelation 12, clothed with the sun, is read in Catholic and Orthodox tradition as both the Church and Mary, assumed into glory and crowned as Queen. Mary's outstretched arms echo her fiat at the Annunciation (Luke 1:38) -- the same surrender that began the Incarnation now reaches its fulfillment in glorification. Psalm 45:9-14 describes the queen standing at the right hand of the king in gold, a passage the Fathers applied to Mary's heavenly enthronement. The Assumption also fulfills the promise of 1 Corinthians 15:54-55: death is swallowed up in victory. Mary, preserved from original sin (the Immaculate Conception), is the first to experience fully the bodily resurrection that awaits all the redeemed. She is, as the Fathers teach, the first fruits of her Son's redemption, the eschatological icon of what the Church will become.

scripture_refs.reason: The woman clothed with the sun, traditionally identified with Mary in her heavenly glorification.
The Ark of the Covenant seen in heaven's temple, immediately preceding the vision of the woman -- patristic typology identifies Mary as the Ark.
Mary's fiat -- her total surrender to God's will -- the posture of openness echoed in her outstretched arms.
The Magnificat: 'the Mighty One has done great things for me' -- the Assumption is the ultimate fulfillment of this canticle.
Arise, O Lord, to your resting place, you and the ark of your might -- applied to Mary as the Ark taken up into heaven.
The queen in gold at the king's right hand, a type of Mary's heavenly enthronement.
Death swallowed up in victory -- Mary's assumption as the first realization of the promise of bodily resurrection.
The longing to be clothed with our heavenly dwelling, realized in Mary's bodily glorification.
